source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
systems which can be divided into three groups: central blacklisting-systems,
eg, in cases of disobedience to administrative ﬁnes or court judgements (thus
a law enforcement tool), local systems of cities that have strong ‘social credit’
components, as well as commercial social credit scores such as Alibaba’s
Sesame Credit.28 These systems inform each other29 as data businesses and
the state have undertaken a state-market symbiosis30 and cooperate in
terms of technical set-up of the system as well as data content.31 The goal
is a uniﬁed system32 (although a uniﬁed single score is not mentioned)
and a digital ﬁle on each actor in society.33 The current reality as seen in
the diﬀerent systems and the envisioned reality of a uniﬁed social credit
system are taken into account in this paper. The CSCS is fed with data from several sources and consequently decides
on the score of corporate entities active on the Chinese market.34 State
oﬃcials then react and take measures that are either sanctions or rewards. Norm abiding corporations can for example obtain tax beneﬁts. The
toolkit of sanctions for deviating corporations is diverse and can lead to
downgrading, blacklisting and/or exclusion from the Chinese market. The CSCS is promoted as an eﬀective tool for ensuring compliance to the
law, contractual obligations and quality standards—something that has
been found lacking by many corporations operating in China.35 Yet, the
CSCS has the potential and the aim of becoming more than that. Internally
it is supposed to build a ‘harmonious Socialist society’.36 As portrayed by
the CCP, the general aim is trust-building on all levels of society, hence on
the economic, the individual and the judicial level as well as enhancing
trust in government branches.37 The terms used in the oﬃcial
documents—trust, trustworthiness, credit, social credit and sincerity—are
not precisely deﬁned. Sometimes the terms refer to abidance to the law,
other times to more blurry concepts such as social morals or family
28
   For a more detailed description of the diﬀerent models: Daithi Mac Sithigh and Mathias Siems, ‘The
   Chinese Social Credit System: A Model for Other Countries?’ (2019) 82(6) The Modern Law Review 1034,
   1048–53. 29
   Yongxi Chen and Anne SY Cheung, ‘The Transparent Self under Big Data Proﬁling: Privacy and Chinese
   Legislation on the Social Credit System’ (2018) 12 Journal of Comparative Law 356, 360–2; See Mac
   Sithigh and Siems, Ibid, 1053 and Creemers (n 15) 24. 30
   Min Jiang and King-Wa Fu, ‘Chinese Social Media and Big Data: Big Data, Big Brother, Big Proﬁt?’
   (2018) 10(4) Policy & Internet 372, 384 and 388. 31
   See Liang and others (n 9) 425. 32
   See Chen and Cheung (n 29) 358; ‘Planning Outline for the Construction of a Social Credit System
   (2014–2020)’ (n 10). 33
   Jiang and Fu (n 30) 381. 34
   For more details on the scope, sources and aims of the (C)SCS please see part II of this article.
